coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive visual sensor for tetracycline in food samples by a double-signal response fluorescent nanohybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Yang, Xiaofeng
Peng, Qianqian
Wang, Fengyi
Zhu, Yue
Hu, Xuan
Zheng, Baozhan
Du, Juan
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit was developed for the detection of tetracycline (TC) in food samples by simply mixing blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (BCDs) and cytidine monophosphate (CMP)/europium coordination polymer nanoparticles (Eu/CMP-cit CPNs) together. In this ratiometric sensor, the emission of BCDs at 465 nm gradually decreased and that of Eu/CMP-cit CPNs at 620 nm significantly increased with the sequential addition of TC upon being excited at 380 nm, accompanyed by a remarkable fluorescence color change from bright blue to red. It was worth mentioning that both of the two components in BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit nanosystem were employed as response signals with different fluorescent signal output. Hence, the sensitivity to TC is greatly enhanced without being affected by other potentially interfering substances. Under the optimized conditions, a good linear relationship between the concentration of TC from 0.05 to 30 μM and the fluorescence intensity ratio of F 620/F 465 was obtained. The detection limit was calculated to be 8 nM, which was much lower than that of most of the previously published methods. Moreover, this proposed nanosystem was also successfully applied to the assay of TC in food samples, including fish, lamb, beef, chicken, pork, egg, milk and honey, and the recovery rates were even comparable to those obtained from high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-based methods. Importantly, a paper-based visual sensor for TC in various food samples was devised by using filter paper embedded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit. The distinct fluorescent color changing from bright blue to red of the filter paper with different concentrations of TC demonstrated a great potential of as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe for the visual detection of TC in complicated real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Visual detection
Paper-based test strip
Tetracycline
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Eu/CMP coordination polymer (Eu/CMP CPNs)
Fabrication of the paper-based visual sensor
Detection of TC in food samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent responses (a) and fluorescence photographs (b) of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit to TC (10 μM) and other antibiotics (20 μM) in HEPES buffer (pH 7.4); From left to right in fluorescence photographs is TC, Blank, STR, CIP, TRI, PEN, TRY, AMI and CEP, respectively. Fluorescent responses of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit to 10 μM TC when the sensor was first mixed with 50 μM physiological substance (c) and ions (d) in HEPES buffer (pH 7.4).
(a) Emission spectra of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit exposed to TC at concentrations from 0 to 30 μM. (b) Plot of F 620/F 465 versus the concentration of TC (Error bars, SD, n = 3).

From top to bottom: the fluorescent color changes of BCDs, Eu/CMP-cit and BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit with the addition of different concentrations of TC under a UV lamp (365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photographs of the corresponding color changes of the test strip after the above processed procedure under 365 nm UV light. The concentration of TC are 0,0.05,0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 1.5 μM,respectively. From (a) to (d) are including the blank chart and the application of the test strips on the milk, pork and honey samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The schematic illustration of TC detection based on the ratiometric fluorescent probe of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit.
Determination of TC in real food samples.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive visual sensor for tetracycline in food samples by a double-signal response fluorescent nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-products, Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-products, Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Chengdu, 610066, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China.
Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit was developed for the detection of tetracycline (TC) in food samples by simply mixing blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (BCDs) and cytidine monophosphate (CMP)/europium coordination polymer nanoparticles (Eu/CMP-cit CPNs) together. In this ratiometric sensor, the emission of BCDs at 465 nm gradually decreased and that of Eu/CMP-cit CPNs at 620 nm significantly increased with the sequential addition of TC upon being excited at 380 nm, accompanyed by a remarkable fluorescence color change from bright blue to red. It was worth mentioning that both of the two components in BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit nanosystem were employed as response signals with different fluorescent signal output. Hence, the sensitivity to TC is greatly enhanced without being affected by other potentially interfering substances. Under the optimized conditions, a good linear relationship between the concentration of TC from 0.05 to 30 μM and the fluorescence intensity ratio of F 620/F 465 was obtained. The detection limit was calculated to be 8 nM, which was much lower than that of most of the previously published methods. Moreover, this proposed nanosystem was also successfully applied to the assay of TC in food samples, including fish, lamb, beef, chicken, pork, egg, milk and honey, and the recovery rates were even comparable to those obtained from high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-based methods. Importantly, a paper-based visual sensor for TC in various food samples was devised by using filter paper embedded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit. The distinct fluorescent color changing from bright blue to red of the filter paper with different concentrations of TC demonstrated a great potential of as-prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe for the visual detection of TC in complicated real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, food safety is a central issue concerning the safety of human being, social economy as well as consumer perception and demand (Grunert, 2005). The residue of antibiotics in food is a significant food safety issue that needs to be addressed. Tetracycline (TC), one of the primarily antibiotics, has been widely used in the therapy of human and animal infections due to its powerful therapeutic effect, good oral absorption, low toxicity and cheap price. However, the irrational abusing of TC may lead to long-term and repeated intake of TC into human body and further do harm to human health (Cox & Popken, 2010). The maximum residue limit of TC in milk was established to be 225 nM and 900 nM by the European Union and the US Food and Drug Administration, respectively (Hou et al., 2016). Hence, there is an urgent need to establish an efficient method for the assay of trace TC in food.

Currently, several methods for TC detection, including liquid chromatography (LC) (Cháfer-Pericás, Maquieira, Puchades, Miralles, & Moreno, 2011; Feng, Wang, Yang, Liu, & Wang, 2016), electrochemistry (D. Chen, Yao, Xie, & Liu, 2014), colorimetric assay (Luo et al., 2015), immunoassay (Song, Yu, et al., 2015b; Y. Chen et al., 2016) and capillary electrophoresis (B. Deng, Xu, Lu, Ye, & Wang, 2012) et al. have been developed. However, most of them demand expensive instruments, inconvenient and time-consuming sample pre-treatment, and the comparatively low sensitivity and selectivity still cannot be ignored. Nowadays, fluorescence based methods have received more and more attention owning to their high-speed, low-cost, high sensitivity and simplicity. Thus, it is promising and of great significance to establish fluorescent measurements for TC detection.

In recent years, lanthanide coordination polymers (LnCPs) constructed by the self-assembly of lanthanide ions and bridging organic ligands have been concerned in many scientific investigations due to the high luminescence quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime, large Stokes shift and sharply spiked emission bands of lanthanide ions (Dong et al., 2015), as well as the superior surface area, tunable pore size and functionalized pore surface of the polymers. Especially, Eu3+-based coordination polymers have the excellent property of red fluorescence emission that is beneficial to be distinguished from background fluorescence and decrease autofluorescence interferences of organism in biological samples, which makes them particularly suitable for visual detection (J. Deng, Shi, & Zhou, 2016; Q. X. Wang et al., 2017). Nevertheless, most of the bridging organic ligands have poor water solubility and biocompatibility, which limits their practical applications in water environment and biological system. As a result, some biomolecules such as nucleobases, nucleosides and nucleotides have shown to be alternative ligands for synthesizing LnCPs (Pu, Ren, & Qu, 2018). However, most studies of LnCPs pay close attention to their exceptional luminescent properties and structure, and pay less attention to the use of fluorescent probes for detection of antibiotics in different food samples (J. Deng, Yu, Wang, & Mao, 2015; Li, Jiang, Zhang, & Shi, 2016; Tong et al., 2018; F. Wang et al., 2018). Tan, Liu, and Chen (2011) investigated the fluorescence response of AMP/Eu coordination polymer to TC. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of AMP/Eu can be dramatically enhanced (30 times) only when TC was incorporated into AMP/Eu network during the preparation process lasting for over 3 h instead of simply mixing TC and AMP/Eu together, which made this assay more complicated and time-consuming.

Moreover, different from traditional single channel-based fluorescent sensor, the ratiometric fluorescent probes possess two channels at two different wavelengths under same excitation wavelength. This sensing platform based on the variation of fluorescence intensity ratio at two different wavelengths can efficiently reduce fluctuations in fluorescence intensity and interferences from instrumental or environmental factors (Tong et al., 2018; Q. X.; Wang et al., 2017). Accordingly, the ratiometric probes with anti-jamming properties provide a more accurate method for practical applications. Carbon quantum dot (CDs), a new class of ‘zero-dimensional’ nanomaterials, have been identified as a rising star in the use of functional fluorescent probes due to their superior merits, including facile synthetic routes, low cost, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, fluorescence tunability and stable luminescent emission et al. (Huang et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2015). In addition, it has been reported that some CDs-based single channel fluorescent sensors have realized the detection of TC (Yang et al., 2014).

Enlightened by the above facts, we have developed a novel strategy to construct a dual-intensity changing ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the assay of trace TC by simply mixing europium coordination polymer nanoparticles (Eu/CMP-cit CPNs) and blue fluorescent CDs (BCDs) together, as shown in Scheme 1 . In this LnCPs, lanthanide coordination polymer self-assembled from the biocompatible and environmental friendly cytosine monophosphate (CMP) and europium ion (Eu3+) was employed as receptor reagent, and citric acid (cit) was chosen to act as ancillary ligand to remove the coordinated water molecules around Eu3+. BCDs with excellent fluorescence properties were synthesized by a convenient solvothermal method. When these two components were mixing together at ambient temperature and pressure, a bright blue fluorescence emission belonged to BCDs can be clearly identified. After the addition of TC, both Eu/CMP-cit CPNs and BCDs responded to TC with different fluorescent signal output, that is, the blue emission of BCDs at 465 nm was correspondingly quenched because of inner filter effect (IFE). At the same time, the characteristic strong red fluorescence of Eu3+ in Eu/CMP-cit CPNs arising from efficient energy transfer from TC to Eu3+ by means of ‘antenna effect’ was opened. It was worth pointing out that BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit CPNs displayed compatible response behavior without mutual interference in fluorescent intensity in the presence of TC. Hence, our work can provide a novel strategy for TC detection with higher sensitivity and more remarkable visual detection effect than single channel approach and traditional dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe in which a stable reference signal and a response signal were included (Song, Yu, et al., 2015a).

What's more, we carried out recovery experiments to assay TC in fish, lamb, beef, chicken, pork, egg, milk and honey samples with satisfactory results, and the obtained recovery rates were even comparable to those obtained from the mature technology of HPLC-based method. Moreover, different fluorescence sensors based on paper or cotton fiber etc. (Han et al., 2018; Kim, Jang, & Lee, 2015; Li et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2018) have been used extensively due to the good sensitivity and specificity, simplicity and visualization of the detection platform. In this work, a filter paper loaded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit has been used to detect trace TC in food samples. As far as we know, this is the first time that the paper-based test strip has been achieved for the detection of TC in complex food samples with good selectivity and performance, and the successful application of detecting trace TC in food samples by filter paper loaded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit manifested the great potential of our visual sensing platform in complicated real samples.

Tetracycline (TC), erythromycin (TRY), ciprofloxacin (CIP), glutathione (GSH), streptomycin sulfate (STR), penicillin (PEN), europium nitrate were purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co.,Ltd. Trimethoprim (TRI), 4-aminoantipyrine (AMI) and cephalexin (CEP) were obtained from Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cytidine 5′-Monophosphate (CMP) was purchased from Bide Pharmatech Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA), ethanol, trisodium citrate and salicylic acid were obtained from Chengdu Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). The filter paper was obtained from Jiao Jie Ltd. (Liaoning, China). Distilled water (≥18.2 MΩ) was used in all experiment procedures by using a Millipore filtration system. Real food samples were purchased from local supermarket.

Photographs were taken by a Sony DSC-WX150 digital camera. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a UV-1100 spectrophotometer (Techcomp (China) Ltd.). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained by a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba JobinYvon) with a Spectral ED (397 nm and 317 nm, S-397 and S-317 nm, Horiba Scientic) as the excitation source and a picosecond photon detection module (PPO-850 Horiba Scientic) as the detector. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Tongda TD-3500 X-ray powder diffractometer (Liaoning, China). The morphology of the coordination polymer was acquired on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, Japan). Separations Module (Waters e2695) and Photodiode Array Detector (Waters 2998) were used in the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). All fluorescent data were recorded in the range of 400–650 nm (λex = 380 nm) and the slit widths (including excitation and emission) were 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Eu/CMP coordination polymer (Eu/CMP CPNs)
ce:section.ce:para: Referring to literature method (Tan et al., 2011), Eu/CMP coordination polymer was synthesized by the following improved processes. Briefly, Eu(NO3)3 aqueous solution (10 mM, 1 mL) was mixed with HEPES buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4, 1 mL) containing CMP (10 mM, 1 mL) under vigorously stirring at room temperature for 3 h. Then, the purification of the white suspension obtained from the above procedures was carried out through centrifuging and washing several times with secondary water. Finally, the white precipitation was re-dispersed in HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4)to form 1 mL Eu/CMP CPNs suspension for further use.

BCDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method in a typical procedure. Salicylic acid (0.2 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 1.0 mL) were well mixed in ethanol (10 mL) and transferred into a 25 mL PTFE reactor. The final product was obtained after reacting at 180 °C for 3 h and cooling to room temperature. The final light yellow product didn't need further purification by filtration and dialysis.

For detection of TC, 990 μL of HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4), 2 μL of BCDs solution, 4 μL of Eu/CMP solution and 4 μL of cit solution (10 mM) were mixed together, which was denoted as BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit. TC solution of different concentrations in the range of 0–5 mM was added into the above solution with a final concentration of 0, 0.05, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 4, 7, 10, 14, 18, 22, 26, 30, 35 and 40 μM, respectively. After reacting for 2 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra was recorded from 400 nm to 650 nm at λex = 380 nm. The emission intensity ratio of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit at 620 nm and 465 nm (F 620/F 465) was used for the quantitative analysis of TC. The same procedures were used for the selectivity experiment, interference study, detection in real samples and so on. All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments.

Eight kinds of food samples (lamb, beef, chicken, pork, eggs, milk, milk powder and honey) were chosen to evaluate the feasibility of the sensor. The pre-treatment procedure referred to National Standard so as to comparing the data from HPLC method and our ratiometric platform under the same sample conditions of treatment. The pre-treatment procedure of milk and honey were according to GB/T 22990–2008 and GB/T 18932.4–2002, respectively. GB/T 20764–2006 was carried on meat, including pork, lamb, fish, beef and chicken. In addition, food samples already treated were mixed with 5 μM TC, and then the process of further analyses were the same as 2.5. Procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based visual sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor solution was prepared by mixing HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4, 9 mL), 400 μL of Eu/CMP solution, 400 μL of cit solution (10 mM) and 100 μL of BCDs solution. Then, the filter paper was cut into small pieces and 40 μL of the sensor solution was dropped on each piece. After the solvent dried naturally, a portable test strip based on BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit was obtained. 35 μL TC solutions with different concentrations were dropped onto test strips. After waiting for 1 min, the test strips were photographed under a 365 nm hand-held UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: TC in various food samples was analyzed by our proposed ratiometric sensing system, HPLC and the as-prepared test strip. In the spike-in experiments, eight kinds of food samples with different concentrations of TC were analyzed by referencing to the 2.5 procedure and the recovery rates were compared with the data from HPLC. In the visual detection experiment, honey, pork and milk were chosen to test the applicability for TC detection in food samples by the as-prepared test strip. First, 35 μL solutions fixed with food samples and different concentrations of TC were dropped onto test strips according to the above 2.7 procedure. Then the fluorescence change of test strips was photographed after illuminated by UV light (365 nm).

Combining the consideration of the excellent luminescent properties of Eu3+ and the satisfactory bio-compatibility and environmental friendliness of the nucleotide-containing coordination polymers, Eu/CMP-cit network was constructed as one of the response signals to assay TC in our work. The Eu/CMP CPNs was constructed by the self-assembly of Eu3+ and CMP in HEPES buffer. As shown in Fig. S1a, the morphology of the as-formed Eu/CMP CPNs characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) clearly reveals a large spatial network structure with nanoscale dimensions. Citric acid (cit) is a polydentate chelating agent which can act as an auxiliary complexing agent to efficiently remove the water molecules coordinating with Eu3+ (Lin, Wu, Schäferling, & Wolfbeis, 2004). Therefore, citric acid was also combined in the nanosystem during the preparation of the probe. For confirming the necessity of incorporating citric acid into the LnCPs, the fluorescence response experiments to TC by utilizing Eu/CMP-cit and Eu/CMP as probes were respectively carried out. The results in Fig. S2 presented a 18 times fluorescence intensity increase when citric acid was incorporated into Eu/CMP-TC network, which was in accordance with the reported results that TC cannot effectively sensitize the emission of Eu/CMP as a light-harvesting unit without the presence of auxiliary ligand cit (Lin et al., 2004; Tan et al., 2013). The coordination of both cit and TC with Eu3+ led to the saturation of the coordination numbers of Eu3+ and the formation of ternary complex emitting strong red fluorescence.

Fluorescent BCDs with a strong blue emission were synthesized by normal hydrothermal carbonization of salicylic acid, which were employed as the other response signal for TC detection. The ratiometric fluorescent TC probe was developed by simply mixing BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit together in aqueous solution. Fig. S1b displayed the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of BCDs, revealing that the synthesized BCDs were monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with an average lattice space of 0.225 nm (Inset of Fig. S1a), which was comparable to the (001) diffraction facets of graphite. The corresponding XRD pattern of BCDs showed a wide diffraction peak at the range of 21.30–24.25°(Fig. S3a), demonstrating the graphitic structure of BCDs (Liu et al., 2018). The obvious broad peak at 3600-3100 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of BCDs (Fig. S3b) should be attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. Moreover, the wide peak at 1610 cm−1 corresponding to the overlapping of CO stretching vibrations and N–H bending vibration, and 1143 cm−1 corresponding to C–O stretching vibrations. The presence of O–H, N–H, CO and C–O stretching vibrations confirmed that BCDs were water soluble.

The possible mutual interference of the two components in the nanosystem was firstly investigated. Fig. S4 showed no detectable variation in the fluorescent spectrum of BCDs after the addition of Eu/CMP-cit nanoparticles, excluding any influence of Eu/CMP-cit on the fluorescence of BCDs. The normalized fluorescent spectra of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit with and without TC were recorded in Fig. S5. BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit displayed one emission centered at 465 nm when excited at 380 nm, which belonged to BCDs. There was no emission of Eu/CMP-cit CPNs can be detected without the addition of TC. In the presence of TC, three new emission peaks at 580 nm, 594 nm and 620 nm were arisen, which were assigned to the characteristic luminescence of Eu3+. Meanwhile, the blue emission of BCDs at 465 nm was slightly decreased with an obvious fluorescence color changing from bright blue to red. Hence, the ratiometric TC sensor based on BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit CPNs was fabricated rapidly and effectively.

To maximize the response effect of the proposed assay for TC, the detection conditions including response time, pH value and ionic strength were explored. As shown in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence ratio (F 620/F 465) of the sensing system was insensitive to pH without the presence of TC, while F 620/F 465 reached a maximum value in the pH range from 8.0 to 10.0 in the presence of TC. The obvious decline of F 620/F 465 in the acid or strong base media can be attributed to the destroyed network structure of Eu/CMP-cit in that pH conditions given that the fluorescence of BCDs was nearly unaffected by pH (Fig. S6b). Furthermore, the protonation of CMP, TC and cit under strong acidic conditions and the formation of europium hydroxide precipitation under strong alkaline conditions should also be considered (Tan et al., 2013). Thus, the pH of 7.4 was chosen as the optical pH value in the subsequent experiments. NaCl solution with the concentration from 0 mM to 600 mM was used to inspect the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescent intensity of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit. Fig. S7 revealed that there was only a negligible decrease in F 620/F 465 of both BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit and BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit + TC systems with the increasing NaCl concentration, revealing the excellent salt stability of the sensor.

Meanwhile, the response time of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit to TC was investigated by monitoring the changes of emission intensity at 465 nm and 620 nm, respectively. From Fig. S8, it can be noticed that the emission intensity of Eu3+ at 620 nm was significantly enhanced while that of BCDs at 465 nm was slightly quenched in 10s with the addition of 20 μM TC. Besides, the response sensitivity of the mixed BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit system to TC was further investigated by studying the variation of F 620/F 465 with the course of time. The result displayed that the ratio of F 620/F 465 reached stable in 2 min after the addition of TC, which was faster than that of the published AMP/Eu-Cit-based method (Tan et al., 2013). In consequence, 2 min was chosen as the optical incubation time for TC assay.

Aiming at evaluating the feasibility of the ratio probe on complex food samples, the selectivity of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit for TC was investigated. Compared with other antibiotics (STR, CIP, TRI, PEN, AMI and TRY), BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit responded specifically to TC with a dramatical change in the ratio of F 620/F 465, owning to the exclusive coordination and light-harvesting capacity of TC to open the fluorescence of Eu3+ (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescence picture in Fig. 1b showed that TC and other antibiotics can be easily distinguished with the naked eye through the visually distinct colour under 365 nm UV lamp.

Moreover, a series of commonly existed potential interferences, including physiological substance (Cys, Ala, Asp, AA, Glu, Glucose, Lys, His, GSH) and ions (Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) were selected to be tested under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the as-mentioned coexisting substances caused no or little influence on the detection of TC even in a 5-fold concentration of TC. The satisfactory results of the analytical performance of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit for TC detection confirmed the feasibility, high sensitivity and specific selectivity of applying the ratiometric probe in complex environments.

Compared with the single signal systems, ratiometric probes with two fluorescent signals had the advantage of low signals of instruments, background and environment. What's more, when compared with other ratiometric sensors composing one response signal and one reference signal, the visual detection performance of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit can be further improved as the two signals responded independently and simultaneously to TC by different signal output. When increasing concentrations of TC were added, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ at 620 nm enhanced continuously while the emission peak of BCDs at 465 nm gradually decreased under the excitation of 380 nm (Fig. 2 a). As typically displayed in Fig. 2b, there was a good linear relationship between the ratio of F 620/F 465 and the concentration of TC from 0.05 μM to 30 μM. The detection limit was calculated to be 8 nM according to a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1, which was comparable to the previously published methods (Table S1).

The controlled experiments were conducted by separately monitoring the changes of the fluorescence intensity of BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit with the addition of different concentrations of TC. The fluorescence intensity of BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit at 465 nm and 620 nm displayed a linear response to TC concentrations in the range of 1 μM–100 μM and 0.05 μM–8.5 μM, and the detection limit was estimated to be 1.3 μM and 12 nM, respectively (Fig. S9). Comparing with BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit, the mixing BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit system displayed a wider linear range from 0.05 μM to 30 μM and a lower detection limit (8 nM) which stressed its excellent performance in TC detection. As shown in Fig. 3 , the gradually switching of the fluorescent color of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit from bright blue to purple, then red, was much more obvious than that of BCDs or Eu/CMP-cit in the same [TC] range from 0 to 15 μM, manifesting its higher sensitivity and reliability for the visual detection and quantification of TC. Moreover, the lacking of visual detection of TC in real samples in the published methods further intensifies the great practical application potential of our method (Table S1).

The intense absorption peaks of BCDs + TC at 290 nm and 370 nm shown in Fig. S10a can be assigned to the characteristic peaks of BCDs and TC, respectively. Comparing with the absorption peaks centered at 290 nm in BCDs and 370 nm in TC, it can be reasonably speculated that the interaction between BCDs and TC was not from the chemical action but from the interaction between the surface groups of BCDs and TC (Hou et al., 2013), which led to a decrease of the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. The excitation and emission spectrum of BCDs as well as the absorption spectrum of TC were gathered in Fig. S11 to further explore the quenching mechanism. There was a good overlap between the absorption spectrum of TC and the excitation spectrum of BCDs, denoting that the quenching behavior of TC toward the fluorescence of BCDs can be attributed to inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of BCDs and Eu/CMP-cit in BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit nanohybrid with and without TC were obtained by exciting at 317 nm and 397 nm, respectively. The same fluorescence lifetime (3.4 ns) of BCDs in BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit and BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit + TC obtained by exciting at 317 nm (Figs. S12a and 12b) also presented a static annihilation process by means of IFE.

Aiming at investigating the fluorescence increase process of Eu3+ induced by TC addition, the UV absorption spectra of TC, Eu/CMP-cit and Eu/CMP-cit + TC were measured (Fig. S11b). Compared to the absorption spectra of TC and Eu/CMP-cit, a new peak emerged at 390 nm in Eu/CMP-cit + TC accompanying with the disappearance of the peak at 365 nm, which indicated a valid chemical coordination between TC and Eu/CMP-cit. For a deeper understanding of the interaction of TC with Eu/CMP-cit, the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit with different [TC] was obtained by exciting at 397 nm. The results shown in Figs. S12c and S12d presented a prolonged fluorescence lifetime of Eu/CMP-cit from 0.03 ms to 0.069 ms with the concentration of TC increasing from 5 μM to 20 μM, which should be attributed to the more efficient replacement of H2O by TC on the unsaturated sites of Eu3+ (Wolfbeis, Dürkop, Wu, & Lin, 2002). The remove of H2O molecules apparently eliminated the quenching effect on the fluorescence of Eu/CMP-cit by energy transformation from the light-harvesting unit TC to Eu3+ and further sensitizing the luminescence of Eu3+ by means of ‘antenna effect’ (Tan et al., 2011).

In order to further exploring the practicability and feasibility of this method in routine assays, our proposed probe was used to study the recoveries of TC in food samples. A series of food samples were chosen from multi-species, including fish, chicken, pork, lamb, beef, egg, milk and honey. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries after the addition of 5 μM TC to different food samples were between 91.0 and 101.8% and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were all lower than 6%, which validated that the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit was practicable and reliable. As we know, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), costly and time-consuming, is a conventional detection method for TC. Aiming to comparing the accuracy of our method with the recommended and adopted HPLC method, the same pretreatments of food samples were accepted according to the National Standard. The results of this comparison listed in Table 1 displayed comparable recovery rates and RSD values with those of HPLC method, confirming the reliability and validity of the BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit sensor in TC detection. Moreover, our proposed fluorescent probe possessed other excellent advantages such as low cost, time-saving, simplicity and visibility, which made it a highly promising fluorescent sensor for monitoring trace TC in different food samples.

To further verify the performance of the as-prepared sensor, the filter paper loaded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit nanosystem was chosen to make a test strip for the detection of TC in milk, pork and honey samples. As shown in Fig. 4 b, c and 4d, a significant colour gradient from bright blue to red can be conveniently detected for each real sample, and the lowest concentration of TC observed by the naked eye could be as low as 0.05 μM, which was much lower than the maximum level of TC permitted by the European Union or the US FDA. Remarkably, the amount of TC concentration read from the colour of the test strip that fixed with milk, honey or pork sample was in good agreement with the cards without the addition of real sample in Fig. 4a, suggesting the great potential of our proposed test strip for practical applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated a sensitive and accurate ratiometric fluorescent method for TC assay based on BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit nanosystem, which presented an independent double-signal response mode. Briefly, TC-induced fluorescence quenching of the BCDs was due to IFE process and the fluorescence opening of Eu/CMP-cit was the result of ‘antenna effect’ deriving from the formation of a stable complex between TC and Eu3+. Apart from the distinct advantage of double-signal response, the presented method was also simple in design, fast in operation, rapid in response and cost-effective. Furthermore, it exhibited an excellent selectivity toward TC over other antibiotics and was not influenced by commonly existed potential interferences. More importantly, as the gradually changing colour of the ratiometric probe from bright blue to red was extraordinary sensitive, it was promising and desirable for BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit to be used as a reliable visual sensor for TC detection. To this end, the fluorescent test strips embedded with BCDs-Eu/CMP-cit were fabricated and showed high sensitivity for detection of TC in food samples. The excellent analytical performance of this method substantially enables it to be applied in TC analysis in complicated food samples.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21876117, U1833124 and U1833202). Prof. Peng Wu in Analytical & Testing Centre at Sichuan University was greatly appreciated for the measurements of fluorescence.
